explanations.1: Friction is a contact force that acts parallel to the interface of two systems and always opposes relative motion or the intent of motion.
explanations.2: Static friction is a responsive force that increases to be equal and opposite to the applied force until the maximum threshold is reached.
explanations.3: The magnitude of kinetic friction is calculated by multiplying the coefficient of kinetic friction by the normal force ($f_k = \mu_k N$).
explanations.4: Terminal velocity is inversely proportional to the square root of the cross-sectional area, so increasing the area increases drag and lowers terminal velocity.
explanations.5: Terminal velocity is reached when the upward drag force equals the downward force of gravity, resulting in zero net force and zero acceleration.
explanations.6: According to Hooke's Law ($F = k\Delta L$), the force is the product of the spring constant and the deformation ($200 \text{ N/m} \times 0.15 \text{ m} = 30 \text{ N}$).
explanations.7: Strain is a unitless quantity defined as the ratio of the change in length ($\Delta L$) to the original length ($L_0$).
explanations.8: Cartilage provides a glassy surface and synovial fluid acts as a natural lubricant, reducing the coefficient of friction to values as low as 0.015.
explanations.9: For large objects at high speeds, the drag force is proportional to the square of the velocity ($F_D \propto v^2$), so doubling the speed quadruples the force.
explanations.10: The Bulk Modulus ($B$) describes a material's resistance to uniform compression and the resulting change in volume.
explanations.11: The normal force on an incline is the component of the weight perpendicular to the surface, which is calculated as $mg \cos(\theta)$.
explanations.12: Shear stress involves forces applied across opposite faces, acting parallel to the surface area rather than stretching or compressing the length.
explanations.13: Stokes' Law applies to small objects at low speeds where the drag force is proportional to the velocity rather than the square of the velocity.
explanations.14: Microscopic friction involves the interaction of atomic lattices which, when rubbed, create vibrations (sound waves) that are converted into thermal energy.
explanations.15: At terminal velocity, the upward drag force must exactly balance the downward weight of the skydiver ($60 \text{ kg} \times 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2 = 588 \text{ N}$).
explanations.16: Tensile strength is defined as the breaking stress that causes an object to fail or sustain permanent structural damage.
